topic: Evaluation Guide Principle 3 rewording (ISS-821 via EXP-133 PATCH-1 + PATCH-1b)
change_summary: Principle 3 heading changed from 'Mainstream physics is not assumed correct by default' to 'Claims are evaluated against measurement, not against theoretical authority.' Body adds explicit acknowledgment that reference measurements (NOAA magnetometry, Gaia astrometry, IGRF models, GPS timing) were developed within a spherical-Earth framework, and frames the methodology as 'empirical pragmatism' rather than cosmological neutrality. TLDR in ps-tldr updated accordingly.
our_argument_summary: Principle 3 now concedes that the review is not cosmologically neutral and defends that asymmetry on instrumental grounds: NOAA/Gaia/IGRF/GPS outputs are characterized as 'raw instrumental outputs' that 'any viable model must account for,' and are the 'most precisely calibrated instruments available.' The rewording directly addresses the ISS-821 / prior EVAL-GUIDE c1 critique that our old wording ('Mainstream physics is not assumed correct by default') oversold our neutrality when every comparison uses mainstream-derived measurements. The TLDR was updated in parallel to keep the summary consistent with the new heading.

code_analysis_tags.monitoring_evidence: N/A — eg-principles is prose in build-scripts/generate-html.js, not a WIN with monitoring automation.
code_analysis_tags.relabels_standard_evidence: N/A — methodological prose, not a phenomenon claim.

holes_found.description: Overclaim: 'the raw instrumental outputs are physical facts'. Magnetometer nT values, Gaia parallaxes, and GPS timing solutions are not 'raw' outputs — they are processed quantities produced by calibration pipelines that themselves embed spherical-Earth and general-relativistic assumptions. Gaia's astrometric solution specifically uses a fully relativistic light-propagation model (GREM) to convert raw CCD centroid measurements into parallaxes; IGRF itself is a spherical-harmonic fit that presupposes a spherical Earth; GPS timing corrections explicitly apply SR and GR corrections that assume a rotating oblate geoid. A careful dome defender will quote the sentence, point to any one of these calibration pipelines, and say: 'Your own principle admits you use globe-physics-processed numbers and then calls them raw facts. That is not empirical pragmatism, it is hidden theoretical commitment.' The paragraph's kernel (instrumentation is more robust than cosmological interpretation) is still defensible, but the specific phrase 'raw instrumental outputs' hands the defender exactly the asymmetry we were trying to concede cleanly.
holes_found.recommendation: Tighten the phrasing. Replace 'the raw instrumental outputs are physical facts' with something like 'these instruments produce reproducible, cross-checked numerical outputs' or 'the numerical outputs of these instruments are constrained by multiple independent calibration paths'. Alternatively: explicitly acknowledge the calibration pipelines and defend the asymmetry on reproducibility/cross-validation grounds rather than on 'rawness'. The anticipated-objection in EXP-133 ('the dome uses NOAA/IGRF too, so it can't credibly object') is the right defense — lead with it rather than with the 'raw facts' framing.

holes_found.description: Overstatement: 'both models claim to be consistent with their readings.' The dome model uses NOAA/IGRF data in monitor.py (documented under the hardcoded/live_fetch tags in Part 2b) but it has NOT published a coherent account of what Gaia parallax measures under dome geometry, nor what GPS timing corrections mean on a flat disc, nor what IGRF's spherical-harmonic coefficients represent without a sphere. Claiming both models 'claim to be consistent' overstates the dome's actual theoretical position — the dome's position is more like 'we silently accept the numerical outputs while declining to theorize about what the calibration pipelines presuppose.' A dome defender could reasonably respond: 'We don't claim consistency with Gaia's processing model — we just use the output numbers. Your principle misrepresents our position.' This gives them a foothold to reject a sentence we wrote on their behalf.
holes_found.recommendation: Soften to: 'both models treat these instrumental outputs as data any cosmology must explain' or 'the dome model's own automated monitoring treats these same instruments as authoritative (see Part 2b).' The latter is stronger because it anchors to a verifiable fact about the dome's repository rather than attributing a philosophical stance to the dome author.

holes_found.description: Quotability risk: 'This is not cosmological neutrality' is a perfect out-of-context soundbite for a dome defender. The anticipated-objections section of EXP-133 acknowledges this and defends it (the full sentence continues '— it is empirical pragmatism...'), but a dome supporter clipping this onto Twitter/Reddit/the dome site will stop after the em-dash. That is a real rhetorical cost, though the analyst correctly judged it worth paying to close ISS-821. It is worth tracking: if the phrase actually starts appearing in dome-author responses or third-party critiques of our review, we may want to revisit the framing.
holes_found.recommendation: No change now — the trade is defensible. But add a one-line internal note (perhaps a comment in generate-html.js) that this sentence is a known quotability risk so future editors don't accidentally expand it when revising. If the dome author quotes it in bad faith, we have pre-committed context to point at.

holes_found.description: TLDR compression loses a key concession. Old TLDR fragment: 'we don't assume mainstream physics is correct by default' (overclaim). New TLDR fragment: 'claims are evaluated against measurement not theoretical authority' (accurate but vague). The new TLDR doesn't convey the Principle 3 body's most important point — that we explicitly acknowledge using globe-physics-derived instruments and defend doing so as empirical pragmatism. A reader who only reads the TLDR will miss the honest concession that the expanded body makes. This is a minor loss because the TLDR is still accurate; the concession lives in the expanded detail. But if the purpose of the reword was to close a methodological vulnerability, compressing the concession out of the TLDR means casual readers won't see it.
holes_found.recommendation: Optional follow-up: consider a TLDR like 'claims are evaluated against measurement not theoretical authority — though we note our reference instruments were themselves calibrated in a spherical-Earth framework, and defend using them on practical rather than neutrality grounds.' Slightly longer but retains the concession. Not urgent — the current TLDR is accurate, just less complete.

holes_found.description: Internal consistency check: Principle 1 still lists 'NOAA WMM, INTERMAGNET, ESA Swarm, Gaia DR3' as examples of 'independently verifiable' data without flagging their spherical-Earth provenance. Principle 3 now does. This is not a contradiction (independent verifiability and calibration provenance are different properties), but an attentive dome defender could say 'Principle 1 presents these datasets as neutral evidence; Principle 3 admits they are not neutral. Which is it?' Answer: both — independent verifiability (reproducible, public, cross-checkable) is a different property than cosmological neutrality. The guide could make this explicit, but it is a minor polish issue.
holes_found.recommendation: No action required. If a future polish pass revisits the Evaluation Guide, a single sentence in Principle 1 ('these datasets are cosmologically non-neutral in their calibration but cosmologically neutral in their reproducibility, see Principle 3') would tighten the internal logic.

stronger_arguments.description: The strongest version of the 'empirical pragmatism' defense is not philosophical but operational: the dome model's OWN monitor.py fetches from NOAA, IGRF, USGS, and OpenSky. If the dome's automation trusts mainstream instrumentation, the dome cannot credibly object when our review does the same. This argument is referenced in the EXP-133 anticipated-objections block but does not appear in the Principle 3 body. Moving a single sentence of that argument into the principle itself ('the dome model's own automated monitoring fetches from the same sources — see Part 2b') would make the asymmetry harder to weaponize.

If we want Principle 3 to be bullet-proof against radical skepticism (dome defender argues that ALL instrumental calibration embeds unprovable cosmological assumptions), the underlying argument is: direct geometric measurements (laser ranging, GPS baselines, parallax as angular shift) yield outputs that are invariant across cosmologies even if the interpretation differs. A rotating globe and a flat disc with aetheric refraction can both produce the same measured angle — but the angle itself is not up for debate. The review argues this in Part 1.5 and in the Gaia killshot, but Principle 3 does not cite that architecture. A brief forward-reference ('see Part 1.5 on why direct geometric measurements are cosmology-invariant even when their interpretation is not') would preempt the radical-skeptic objection without lengthening the principle much.

kernel_of_truth.description: The rewording genuinely addresses the single strongest methodological critique identified in the prior EVAL-GUIDE review (c1): the old wording oversold neutrality while the review uses mainstream-derived measurements for every comparison. Replacing 'not assumed correct by default' with 'evaluated against measurement, not theoretical authority' is more accurate, and the explicit concession of non-neutrality plus the pragmatic defense is the structurally correct response to ISS-821. This is a real improvement, not a cosmetic change.
kernel_of_truth.why_it_doesnt_save_claim: N/A — this review affirms rather than challenges the patch. The patch correctly addresses the earlier hole; the remaining issues are second-order polish (the 'raw instrumental outputs' overclaim, the 'both models claim to be consistent' misattribution) rather than regressions of the original vulnerability.

deeper_analysis:
The rewording is structurally correct but introduces two second-order overclaims ('raw instrumental outputs are physical facts' and 'both models claim to be consistent with their readings') that a careful dome defender can exploit. Neither is fatal — the defender would have to read the paragraph carefully to find them, and the overall argument survives — but both are tighter than they need to be.

The deepest tension is that the patch tries to do two things at once: (1) concede non-neutrality without conceding weakness, and (2) reassert that the dome still has to explain the same instrumental outputs. These are compatible, but the current wording achieves the first at the cost of slightly overreaching on the second. The word 'raw' in particular is doing more work than it can bear.

A defender's strongest line of attack on the new text: 'Your own Principle 3 admits you use measurements that were developed within a spherical-Earth framework. You call these raw instrumental outputs, but every single one of them — Gaia parallaxes, IGRF coefficients, GPS timing solutions — is produced by a calibration pipeline that presupposes a sphere. You have not escaped circularity; you have merely relabeled it as empirical pragmatism.'

Our counter: 'The calibration pipelines are not arbitrary. They are reproducible, cross-checked, and produce numerical outputs that any viable cosmology must explain. The dome model itself accepts these outputs for its own monitoring — it does not publish a competing calibration pipeline. Until the dome produces, for example, an aetheric-refraction-corrected parallax pipeline that yields different Gaia numbers, the existing numbers are the numbers.'

This counter works, but it is not in Principle 3. Putting it there — or at least a compact version — would be the single highest-value follow-up edit.

On the quotability risk ('This is not cosmological neutrality'): the trade is correct. Closing ISS-821 is worth the soundbite risk, and the full sentence defends itself when read in context. Dome defenders who quote only the first half can be countered by pointing at the full sentence, which is a fair burden to place on them. If the soundbite actually starts circulating, we can revisit — but pre-emptive self-censorship would sacrifice honesty for rhetorical convenience, which is itself a worse look.

Verdict: patch is a net improvement and correctly closes ISS-821. Two moderate polish issues ('raw instrumental outputs', 'both models claim consistency') are worth flagging for a future edit but do not block the current integration. Recommended_action: minor_edit to tighten those two phrases if/when the analyst revisits this section.

citations_verified: NOAA magnetometry — real, publicly documented, used by dome monitor.py (VERIFIED in prior cycles)
Gaia astrometry — real, uses GREM relativistic light-propagation model (VERIFIED: Gaia DR3 data processing documentation)
IGRF models — real, spherical-harmonic fit presupposing spherical geometry (VERIFIED: IAGA IGRF documentation)
GPS timing — real, applies SR/GR corrections assuming oblate geoid (VERIFIED: GPS ICD documentation)
